place: Fort Covington CDP, New York, New York

facts.text: Fort Covington’s border inspection station, built in 1932, is one of seven New York stations constructed between 1931 and 1934 to curb alcohol smuggling.
The station uniquely controls a border that spans both land and water, a feature shared by only a handful of U.S. customs posts.
Fort Covington sits just south of the Canada–United States border along the Salmon River, with the town’s northern half forming the CDP’s core.
New York State Route 37 cuts east–west through the CDP, linking Fort Covington to Malone 15 miles southeast and Massena 21 miles west.
The Fort Covington–Dundee Border Crossing connects Dundee Road in the hamlet to Quebec Route 132, which follows the south shore of the St. Lawrence River.
Herbert Donovan’s research collection at St. Lawrence University documents Fort Covington’s history through correspondence, maps, and photographs, preserving the town’s heritage.
